ambit — embedding-space occupancy

ambit / docs / design / visualization study theme

How a dataset occupies its embedding space

An embedding model maps every item in a corpus to a point in a high-dimensional space, and a 2-D projection lets us look at the cloud those points form. But a scatter of 12,000 dots only half-answers the real question: how much of the embedding space does this dataset actually occupy, and where are its hotspots and voids? A corpus can crowd a single megacluster while leaving most of the reachable space empty; it can wrap a donut of coverage around an interior hole the data never learned to fill; it can over-represent one region and starve another relative to the base embedding space it was sampled from. ambit reads that occupancy structure directly — density and its peaks, coverage and its concave reach, voids and sparsity, and the signed surplus / deficit against a reference. Below: eleven first-principles views of the same canonical projection, each surfacing one property of how the cloud fills the plane. denser / better-covered = warmer; crowded-beyond-reference / hole = read by direction.

The canonical sample · one projected embedding cloud, shared across all 11 views

points12,000 embedded items (1 per row of the corpus)
source dim768-d sentence embeddings (transformer CLS-pooled)
projectorUMAP · n_neighbors=15 · min_dist=0.1 · cosine
structure1 megacluster + 2 satellites · 1 thin bridge · diffuse halo
voids2 interior holes (1 large donut, 1 minor) · sparse outlier tail
coverageoccupied area ≈ 61% of convex hull (concave reach)
density peakmegacluster core ≈ 18× the diffuse-halo density
referencebase embedding space (full 240k-item corpus KDE) for over/under

Every figure below depicts the same synthetic projection — a 2-D UMAP of 12,000 768-dimensional embeddings with a deliberately legible occupancy structure: one dense megacluster (its core ≈ 18× the diffuse-halo density), two smaller satellites joined to it by a single thin bridge, a diffuse halo of sparse outliers, and two interior voids (one large donut hole, one minor pocket). The occupied region reaches ≈ 61% of its convex hull — the rest is concave bite-outs and the empty interior. The comparison views score this cloud against a base embedding space (a KDE over the full 240k-item corpus) to read where the dataset over-occupies and where it under-occupies the space it was drawn from.

VIZ 01Isodensity contour reliefkde contoursOpens the study with the continuous shape of occupancy: nested closed level-sets read the cloud like a topographic map, so broad gentle slopes (diffuse halo) and tight stacked rings (the megacluster hotspot) separate at a glance, and the outermost ring is the dataset's effective footprint.
contour ring (density quantile) peak ring (accent spine) void / zero-density gap density ramp cool→hot
Reveals: the dataset's occupancy relief: tight stacked rings over the megacluster peak (a sharp hotspot) vs broad gentle bands over the diffuse halo, with the outermost closed band tracing the effective support boundary and the paper-colored gap marking the dataset's most conspicuous coverage void.
VIZ 02Hexbin occupancy heatmaphexbinRestates the same field as a discrete histogram so density becomes countable: each hex tints by its local point count, hotspots read as saturated clusters, connective tissue as mid-tint, and voids drop out as bare paper cells.
hex cell (binned count) hottest cell (accent outline) empty cell (void = bare paper) 123 count label (mono)
Reveals: The coarse occupancy histogram across the whole plane: the hottest cell sits on the megacluster core, mid-tint cells trace the bridge and halo, and the two interior voids render as the literal absence of ink (empty cells).
VIZ 03Local-density rank dot fieldgraded scatterShows the raw cloud itself but grades every point by its local neighborhood density, so the viewer reads the literal scatter AND the hotspot structure at once and can ask per point 'in a crowd or alone?'.
point graded by local density dense core (hot) sparse / outlier (faint) densest point (accent) largest coverage void
Reveals: Both the literal shape of the data and its density grading in one mark: cores glow hot, peripheries cool, and the lone outlier-tail dots in the voids stand out as faint, isolated points far from any pile-up — an outlier read as neutral, never bad.
VIZ 04Density-peak prominence rankingpeak ridgeReduces the smooth field to its named, ranked peaks — each local maximum a numbered summit with a topographic prominence value — answering how many distinct clusters the dataset has, where they are, and which dominates.
peak (sized by prominence) dominant peak P1 (accent) context isodensity ring prominence cutoff (dashed)
The dataset’s modal structure: how many distinct hotspots exist, ranked by prominence, separating the one true megacluster (P1) from the two minor satellite peaks (P2, P3) and noise bumps that fall below the prominence cutoff.
VIZ 05kNN manifold graphknn meshDraws the literal connective tissue of occupancy: a k-NN graph wires dense regions into a tight mesh and thins to single strands across the sparse frontier, so clusters read as knots, voids as gaps the edges route around, and the bridge as a lone thread.
short / dense edge (hot) long / frontier edge (fades to faint) longest edge / bridge (accent spine) void outline · edges route around node dot · one per embedded item
The dataset’s connectivity: clusters surface as dense mesh knots (the megacluster wires into the tightest weave, the two satellites into looser ones), the two voids read as clean gaps the edges route around rather than cross, and the single most-strained edge — the longest retained span — flags the weakest stitch: the lone bridge thread between megacluster and the mid-right knot. Edge length doubles as a local-density read — hot short edges pack the cores, faint long strands stretch the sparse frontier.
VIZ 06Bridge betweenness chokepointsbetweennessThe complement of the dense mesh: it isolates the load-bearing strands whose removal would split the manifold into disconnected territories, pinpointing exactly where occupancy is a few islands joined by thin land-bridges.
edge (width = betweenness) critical bridge (accent spine) articulation point (caution ring) cluster medoid coverage void
Reveals: The manifold's chokepoints — the few high-betweenness edges whose removal disconnects whole territories. The single critical bridge (b=0.96) between the megacluster and the mid-right knot is the load-bearing isthmus: cut it and occupancy fractures into separate islands. Its endpoint medoids and the thin bridge beads are flagged as articulation points (caution rings) — the honest fragility of a manifold held together by one thread.
VIZ 07Alpha-hull reach boundaryalpha shapeTraces the true outer extent of the occupied region — the concave frontier that hugs the cloud and carves in at the deep notches where data stops — distinguishing how far the data reaches from how much it actually wraps.
alpha-hull boundary (accent) convex hull (dashed reference) point (neutral) hull-touching vertex
Reveals: the dataset's concave frontier — the alpha boundary hugs the cloud and carves into the peninsulas and fjords where points stop, while the dashed convex hull floats outside. The gap between them is the inflated reach the data does not actually occupy (~39% of hull area).
VIZ 08Empty-disc void detectionvoid discsSurfaces the largest empty pockets INSIDE the occupied region — the holes the data forgot to cover — by inscribing the maximal point-free circles, naming where the dataset has interior coverage gaps versus a solidly filled core.
largest void (accent spine) secondary void (neutral) void center + radius frontier (dashed reference)
Reveals: the dataset's interior holes — the maximal empty discs name where coverage is missing. The largest void (the donut hole punched through the diffuse region) is the worst gap, with a smaller secondary void near the upper cluster, both distinguished from exterior emptiness by the dashed frontier.
VIZ 09Nearest-neighbor sparsity fieldnn ringsRenders local sparsity as a continuous quantity: at every point, how far is its nearest neighbor — tight clusters read as tiny glyphs, isolated frontier and bridge points read as wide rings reaching into the emptiness, surfacing the thinning gradient a binary hull misses.
NN ring (radius = sparsity) typical spacing top-decile sparse (bad) median spacing (dashed) point (1-NN center)
Reveals: local sparsity as a continuous field — the dense megacluster core wears tiny-to-absent rings while the top-decile sparsest points on the frontier, the load-bearing bridge, and the outlier tails wear wide rings that visually claim the void around them. The ring radii thin smoothly from core to edge, exposing the coverage gradient and the two interior holes that a binary occupancy hull would flatten to a single yes/no.
VIZ 10Differential log-ratio densitysurplus/deficitOpens the comparison arc with the signed surplus/deficit field against the base embedding space: hot cells are regions this dataset crowds beyond the reference, cold cells are regions it abandons — the clearest 'what does it emphasize and what does it neglect' read.
surplus L>0 (crowds beyond ref, toward good) deficit L<0 (abandons ref space, toward bad) parity L≈0 (neutral, empty) L=0 parity boundary (dashed)
Reveals: where the dataset over- vs under-occupies the base space. The megacluster reads as the hottest surplus — cells this dataset crowds far beyond reference (peak L≈+2.5), with the mid-right knot a milder surplus (L≈+0.8) — while the donut void and the abandoned upper-left reference reach read cold (L≈−2.6): space the base corpus populates but this dataset deserts. Satellite A reads as a moderate surplus (L≈+1.5) — a second region this dataset crowds beyond reference; the dashed L=0 boundary rings the occupied structure, and parity with the base stays an empty neutral midpoint — surplus greens, deficit reds.
VIZ 11Q-Q occupancy curveq-q curveCloses the study with the compact distributional read: whether the dataset's occupancy follows the same shape as the reference or systematically over/under-concentrates across the whole density range — tail behavior in one glance.
Q-Q curve (data line) identity diagonal (parity) over-concentrated (above) decile dot extreme-tail endpoint
Reveals: whether the dataset is more concentrated or more even than the base. The curve bows above the diagonal at every decile, so this dataset crowds its dense regions (the megacluster) harder than the reference — peakier occupancy, +0.10 signed area of over-concentration. The extreme-tail endpoint dots mark where it lives: the densest 1% (top-right, the megacluster core) and the sparsest 1% (bottom-left, the voids and coverage gaps). Parity would ride the dashed identity line.
ambit · embedding-space occupancy visualization study · eleven first-principles views of one canonical 2-D projection (12,000 items · 768-d source · UMAP), each surfacing one occupancy property — density, coverage, voids, or surplus/deficit against the base space. Self-contained · theme-adaptive (16 schemes) · Tufte line-art, token-only marks.
